question: How to brew compost tea?
title: How to Brew Compost Tea: A Simple Method to Multiply Your Soil Biology | Scotty's Gardening Lab
metaDescription: Compost tea is a microbial-rich liquid brewed from finished compost and water. Here's how to make it at home with an air pump, and how to use it in your garden.
content:
I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book. That's how a lot of things start for me, I read something that sounds almost too good to be true, then I try it and the book was right.

Compost tea is basically a method of multiplying the microbial community that's already in your finished compost. You take a scoop of good living material, compost full of bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, drop it in water, add a little food for those organisms, and aerate it for 24 hours. What you get is a microbial-rich liquid you can put directly on your soil or spray on plant leaves. You're scaling up the biology.

The math behind this is pretty simple. A gallon of compost tea brewed from half a cup of good compost can inoculate a much larger area than you could cover by spreading that same compost. You're not diluting the biology, you're amplifying it. The aerobic conditions during brewing let beneficial bacteria and fungi multiply fast. More organisms, same 24 hours.

---

**The short version:** Put quality finished compost in a bucket with dechlorinated water, add a small amount of molasses, run an aquarium air pump for 24 hours. Apply immediately, the organisms lose viability fast once the oxygen stops. Drench soil around plants or spray the leaves. Don't let it sit past 36 hours or anaerobic bacteria take over.

---

## What's Actually Happening in the Bucket

Before I walk y'all through the steps, let me explain the biology. Because if you understand what's happening, you'll make better decisions all the way through.

Finished compost is full of microorganisms, bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, all in a relatively slow-active state because the composting process has consumed most of the available food. When you put a scoop into warm water and bubble air through it, you're changing three things at once: you're hydrating the organisms, giving them a medium to multiply and move through, and adding the oxygen aerobic organisms need to reproduce.

The molasses is food, a simple sugar that aerobic bacteria metabolize quickly to fuel reproduction. In 24 hours under good conditions, a bacterial population can double many times over. You're going from a dormant scoop of compost to an actively growing community many times larger.

KIS Organics, one of the more thorough practical resources on actively aerated compost tea, puts the goal plainly: increase the diversity and biomass of beneficial aerobic microorganisms in the soil and on the leaf surface. More diversity, more biomass, in liquid form so it can reach roots easily.

## What You Need

This is one of the more accessible things in the biological gardening toolkit. You really don't need anything fancy.

**Compost.** Non-negotiable. Your tea is only as good as your compost. I use my own hot compost that's fully cured, worm phase completed, good structure, that rich dark earthy smell. If your compost is immature or smells off, your tea reflects that. The biology in the compost is your whole starting point.

**Water.** Tap water works but needs to be dechlorinated first. Chlorine is designed to kill bacteria, and you don't want it killing your beneficial organisms during brewing. Let tap water sit uncovered for a few hours, chlorine off-gases pretty quickly. Chloramine, which some municipalities use, doesn't off-gas as easily and may need a dechlorination product. Rainwater is ideal if you've got it.

**An air pump and airstone.** A basic aquarium air pump with an airstone, the kind for a fish tank, is all you need for batches up to about five gallons. The airstone distributes bubbles evenly through the water. You want high dissolved oxygen throughout, not just at the surface.

**A bucket.** Five-gallon buckets work well for garden-scale brewing. Scale up proportionally for larger areas, same ratios, bigger vessel.

**Microbial food.** Unsulfured blackstrap molasses is the most common, about one to two tablespoons per five gallons. Some folks also add liquid kelp or fish emulsion for additional mineral support and to bring in more fungal diversity. For most home gardens, molasses alone is fine.

## The Process, Step by Step

Fill your bucket with dechlorinated water at roughly room temperature. Too cold slows microbial reproduction. Above 80 degrees Fahrenheit and you start encouraging the wrong organisms. Aim for 65 to 75 degrees.

Add your compost, about half a cup to one cup per five gallons of water. I put mine in a mesh bag or an old onion bag so the compost doesn't clog application equipment later. Drop the bag in the water.

Add your food. One to two tablespoons of unsulfured blackstrap molasses per five gallons. Stir to dissolve.

Drop in the airstone, run the tubing up to your pump, turn it on. You want vigorous aeration, the water should be actively moving and bubbling throughout. Dead spots mean reposition the airstone or add another one.

Brew for 24 hours. That's the sweet spot. KIS Organics and most AACT practitioners agree that past 36 hours, even in well-aerated systems, anaerobic bacteria start gaining ground. Once anaerobes take over, you've gone from beneficial to potentially harmful.

Apply immediately. Compost tea starts losing its microbial population within hours of the pump turning off. Don't brew it and let it sit for days. Brew it, use it, same day.

## How to Apply It

Soil drench is the most common method. Pour or pump the finished tea directly around the base of plants where you want to build root-zone biology. For beds you're establishing or trying to rehabilitate, apply generously and let it soak in.

Foliar spray is the second option. Diluted compost tea applied to leaf surfaces can introduce bacteria and fungi that compete with pathogens. Spray in the early morning when temperatures are cooler and the tea stays wet longer on the leaves.

For garden beds I'm establishing or top-dressing, I apply compost tea the same week I'm putting down compost or mulch. The liquid biology gets the microbial community moving fast, while the solid material provides structure and ongoing nutrition for the growing community.

Cured compost that I've let my worms work through is my go-to starting material for tea. The worm-cured phase produces particularly fungal-rich material with a more diverse organism community. That diversity translates directly into a more diverse tea.

Gabe Brown's framework for living soil applies here: you're trying to build the web of life in your soil, not just add one input. Compost tea done right isn't a fertilizer. It's a biological inoculant, a way of establishing and extending the microbial community that makes your soil actually alive.

Y'all give this a try. The equipment is cheap, the process is simple, and the results in a depleted garden are real.

---

## Sources

1. KIS Organics, The Ultimate Compost Tea Guide: https://www.kisorganics.com/blogs/news/the-ultimate-compost-tea-guide
2. Mother Earth News, How to Make Aerated Compost Tea: https://www.motherearthnews.com/organic-gardening/gardening-techniques/aerated-compost-tea-zebz1307zsie/
3. Homestead and Chill, How to Make Compost Tea to Fertilize Your Garden (Aerated): https://homesteadandchill.com/actively-aerated-compost-tea/
4. Rural Sprout, How to Brew Aerated Compost Tea: https://www.ruralsprout.com/aerated-compost-tea/
5. Cornell University Composting Science, Compost Microorganisms: https://compost.css.cornell.edu/microorg.html

scottyQuotes: Compost tea is super easy to make. You just take a scoop of compost, good living soil, I took the earthworms out of it, and then you add some good water. I use rainwater. Bubble it up for 24 hours with an aquarium air pump. What you get is a microbial-rich solution.
I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book. And then I tried it and discovered the book wasn't exaggerating.
After the hot composting phase, I let the worms work through it. That cured, worm-worked material is my go-to for compost tea. The diversity in that material becomes the diversity in your tea.
